results.snippet: Once the physical quantity is measured, then the quantum state changes from a probabilistic description to a specific one, and that can only be one of the ones that was "allowed" by the physical situation. There is one other thing we should say about observable values in quantum theory before moving on.

results.title: Chapter 4: Energy and the Hamiltonian - Quantum Mechanics
results.snippet: We will reintroduce the Hamiltonian operator (the total-energy operator), in the context of mechanics, its associated eigenvalue equation, and discuss the important notion of bound and non-bound states, and their distinct description within quantum mechanics. 4.1 The Hamiltonian What different types of energy can a particle have?

results.title: Understand eigenstates and Eigenvalues
results.snippet: When you measure a property of a quantum system, like its energy or spin, the outcome isn't random. Quantum mechanics predicts that the only possible results you can get are specific, discrete values. These special values are called eigenvalues. Each eigenvalue corresponds to a specific state of the system, known as an eigenstate. Think of it like this: a guitar string can only vibrate at ...

results.title: 2.4 Properties of Energy Eigenstates ‣ Chapter 2 Quantum Mechanics in ...
results.snippet: If the particle's energy is measured, its state will instantly and non-deterministically collapse into one of the energy eigenstates. For example when measuring the energy of the two-energy state above, we can tell from the coefficients that we have 50 % probability to measure E 1 and 50 % probability to measure E 2.

results.title: CHM 305 - Lecture 5 - Quantum Operators and Eigenfunctions
results.snippet: In quantum mechanics, eigenvalues are always real and represent measurable values of the observable. Using the Hamiltonian operator as an example, our observable is energy and the eigenvalue En represents the energy of the system when it is in the ψn(x) eigenstate (or stationary state).

results.title: Eigenvalues and Eigenfunctions - HyperPhysics
results.snippet: To obtain specific values for physical parameters, for example energy, you operate on the wavefunction with the quantum mechanical operator associated with that parameter. The operator associated with energy is the Hamiltonian, and the operation on the wavefunction is the Schrodinger equation.

results.title: Quantum Mechanics - University of Tennessee
results.snippet: In quantum mechanics, a measurement of an observable yields a value, called an eigenvalue of the observable. Right after the measurement, the state of the system is an eigenstate of the observable, which means that the value of the observable is exactly known.

C002.claim: Eigenstates are mathematically defined by the equation $\hat{A} | \psi \rangle = a | \psi \rangle$, where $\hat{A}$ is an observable operator and $a$ is the definite measurement value.

results.title: Bra-ket notation - Wikipedia
results.snippet: The mathematical structure of quantum mechanics is based in large part on linear algebra: Wave functions and other quantum states can be represented as vectors in a separable complex Hilbert space. (The exact structure of this Hilbert space depends on the situation.) In bra-ket notation, for example, an electron might be in the "state" |ψ .

results.title: Eigenvectors and Eigenvalues: The Quantum Computing Secret Sauce
results.snippet: Eigenvectors and Eigenvalues: The Quantum Computing Secret Sauce Ever wondered what makes quantum computers so powerful? At the heart of quantum mechanics—and thus quantum computing—lies a pair of mathematical twins: eigenvectors and eigenvalues. Don't worry, no advanced linear algebra background needed. Let's break it down with everyday analogies.

results.title: Hydrogen atom - Wikipedia
results.snippet: December 31, 2025 - Again the Dirac equation may be solved analytically in the special case of a two-body system, such as the hydrogen atom. The resulting solution quantum states now must be classified by the total angular momentum number j (arising through the coupling between electron spin and orbital angular momentum). States of the same j and the same n are still degenerate. Thus, direct analytical solution of Dirac equation predicts 2S( ... 1/2) levels of hydrogen to have exactly the same energy, which is in a contradiction with observations (Lamb–Retherford experiment).

results.title: Quantum harmonic oscillator - Wikipedia
results.snippet: 1 week ago - First, the energies are quantized, meaning that only discrete energy values (integer-plus-half multiples of ħω) are possible; this is a general feature of quantum-mechanical systems when a particle is confined. Second, these discrete energy levels are equally spaced, unlike in the Bohr model of the atom, or the particle in a box.

results.title: Wave function - Wikipedia
results.snippet: 3 weeks ago - In the case of non separable Hamiltonians, energy eigenstates are said to be some linear combination of such states, which need not be factorizable; examples include a particle in a magnetic field, and spin–orbit coupling.

C006.claim: Most quantum states are superpositions of eigenstates, not eigenstates themselves.

results.title: Quantum superposition - Wikipedia
results.snippet: Any quantum state can be expanded as a sum or superposition of the eigenstates of an Hermitian operator, like the Hamiltonian, because the eigenstates form a complete basis:

results.title: 3.8: Eigenstates and Eigenvalues - Physics LibreTexts
results.snippet: Mar 13, 2026 · This page explains eigenstates and eigenvalues of Hermitian operators in quantum mechanics, where eigenstates remain proportional after the operator acts on them, and eigenvalues are real.

results.title: particle physics - Is "quantum superposition" just a fancy ...
results.snippet: Dec 25, 2017 · For a particle in a box, we can have an eigenstate of the hamiltonian, which is a standing wave, or an eigenstate of momentum, which is a traveling wave. The standing wave is a superposition of traveling waves, and the traveling wave is a superposition of standing waves.

results.title: Interpretation of superposition of eigen states and ...
results.snippet: It is obvious that the superposition of eigenstates is not an alternative eigenstate. If the super-position is mathematically possible, then we inevitably encounter the measurement problem. However if not so, the measurement problem should be assessed as having been a misled one from the outset.

results.title: Lecture 14: Superposition & Time-Dependent Quantum States
results.snippet: Time-dependence of Superpositions A particle can be in a superposition of states that have different energies. This superposition is still a solution of the time-dependent SEQ, but not of the time-independent SEQ, because two different E’s are involved.

C010.claim: Eigenstates are not 'building blocks' of reality but mathematical constructs for predicting measurement outcomes.

results.title: 3.8: Eigenstates and Eigenvalues - Physics LibreTexts
results.snippet: This page explains eigenstates and eigenvalues of Hermitian operators in quantum mechanics, where eigenstates remain proportional after the operator acts on them, and eigenvalues are real. It covers …

results.title: What is Eigenstate? Meaning, Examples, Use Cases, and How to Measure It?
results.snippet: Analogy: Like a tuning fork that vibrates at only one pitch when struck; the pitch is the eigenvalue and the fork's vibration pattern is the eigenstate. Formal technical line: In linear algebra and quantum mechanics, an eigenstate is an eigenvector of an operator with a corresponding eigenvalue, satisfying O|ψ> = λ|ψ>.

results.title: [2604.11872] Eigenstate thermalization - arXiv.org
results.snippet: We provide a pedagogical introduction to eigenstate thermalization. This phenomenon, which occurs in generic quantum systems, allows one to understand why thermalization takes place in isolated systems under unitary dynamics. We motivate eigenstate thermalization using random matrix theory and discuss recent complementary results for the volume-law entanglement entropy of Haar-random states ...

results.title: Eigenvectors and Eigenvalues: The Quantum Computing Secret Sauce
results.snippet: Ever wondered what makes quantum computers so powerful? At the heart of quantum mechanics—and thus quantum computing—lies a pair of mathematical twins: eigenvectors and eigenvalues.

results.title: What Is An Eigenstate In Superposition Visualization? - Quantum Tech ...
results.snippet: We'll explain what an eigenstate is and how it serves as a fundamental building block in quantum mechanics.
